description: Kashi knows her as the goddess who feeds the world, and the Annapurna Chalisa carries that promise into the home. Forty chaupais make up the body of the hymn, with framing dohas rounding out the recitation, and the count of forty is exactly where the word chalisa comes from. It grew out of folk tradition rather than any single scripture, sung by households facing want. Those who recite it ask Annapurna to drive poverty, hunger, and scarcity from the house, to fill it instead with food, wealth, and prosperity, and to grant jnana and the detachment of vairagya alongside. Tuesdays and Fridays are the usual days, and Navratri and Annapurna Jayanti the special ones. A full reading runs about eight minutes. The page below gives you the Devanagari text, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a PDF to keep offline.

Ten verses, no ritual, no festival calendar to track except one. These selected lines from the Ashtavakra Gita sit at the sharp end of Advaita teaching, where the sage tells King Janaka that bondage was only ever an idea. They point straight at Self-realization through knowledge, asking the seeker to drop all identification with body and mind and to recognize the Self as pure witness, infinite, already free. There is nothing to attain here, only an illusion to see through. Guru Purnima is the day most associated with sitting down to this text, fitting for a dialogue about the awakening teacher. Reading it through takes around eight minutes. On this page you will find the verses in Devanagari, a plain transliteration, the meaning explained in Hindi, and a PDF you can download and return to.

Arjuna asks to see, and what he sees breaks him open. Chapter eleven of the Bhagavad Gita, the Vishwarupa Darshana Yoga of the Mahabharata, is where Krishna unveils his cosmic form, mouths and worlds without end, and speaks the line that has echoed for millennia: Kalo'smi Lokakshayakrit Pravruddho. Across fifty-five verses the chapter teaches that the same God creates and destroys all things, and that the vision of his infinite form is reserved for supreme devotion alone. Wednesdays suit its recitation, and Janmashtami most of all. Expect to spend roughly twenty-four minutes with it. The page presents the full Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow along, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF for offline reading.

Three verses, drawn from the Rudrayamala Tantra, aimed at the guardian who stands at the edge of fear. The Bhairava Gayatri is a Vedic-style meditation mantra invoking Kala Bhairava, and the tradition turns to it to strip away fears and place the devotee under his protection. It carries the promise of the ashta-siddhis, the eight supernatural powers, and it is held to be especially strong for those haunted by the dread of untimely death. Tuesdays and Saturdays are the days for it; Kalashtami and Maha Shivaratri the nights when its chanting is considered most potent. A complete recitation takes about four minutes. Here you can read it in Devanagari, in plain transliteration, with the Hindi meaning laid out, and download a PDF to chant offline whenever you wish.

Offer Shiva a single bilva leaf and the merit, the tradition says, rivals the greatest of donations. The Bilvashtakam, eight verses from the body of traditional Sanskrit hymnody, is built around exactly that idea. Reciting it is believed to wash away the sins of three lifetimes, to carry merit on a par with a Kashi pilgrimage or an Ashvamedha Yagna, and to secure Shivaloka after death. Monday is its natural day, and Maha Shivaratri together with the month of Shravan its high season, when bilva worship reaches its peak. The eight verses move quickly, about four minutes in all. You will find the complete hymn below in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth explaining each verse, and a PDF download for chanting away from the screen.

Four abodes, one short prayer. The Char Dham Stotram gathers the great pilgrimage sites into five compact verses, drawn from Purana tradition, and addresses Vishnu in all his forms at once. Devotees recite it for spiritual growth and for the divine protection and auspiciousness it is said to bring, and the tradition holds that it removes sins and bestows spiritual merit on the one who chants it. Thursdays and Mondays are the days kept for it, with Devuthani Ekadashi and the Rath Yatra marking the festival occasions. With only five verses, the reading takes about three minutes, which makes it as suited to a daily habit as to a special day. The page carries the full Devanagari text, a transliteration in plain letters, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF to download.

Twelve names, five verses, one guru who is Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva together. The Dattatreya Dwadash Nama Stotram comes from the Dattatreya Purana and gathers the twelve sacred names of Lord Dattatreya into a form short enough for daily use. Chanting it is said to bring sarva-siddhi, every kind of supernatural accomplishment, to remove sins and grant jnana-siddhi, the siddhi of spiritual knowledge, and to keep the devotee under the constant grace of the guru lineage. Thursdays and Wednesdays are its days, while Datta Jayanti and Guru Purnima are the festivals that call for it. The five verses take roughly three minutes to recite. On this page the stotram appears in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a PDF you can download and keep for offline recitation.

Shiva is said to have declared it plainly: without the Kunjika, the Chandi Path bears no fruit. The Siddha Kunjika Prayoga, from the Rudra Yamala Tantra and bound to the Devi Mahatmya, is the key that opens the full power of the Durga Saptashati. Eight verses pack in the most potent Shakti bija mantras in concentrated form, and the tradition treats it as complete in itself, standing in for the Kavach, Argala, Kilak, and Rahasya all at once. Tuesdays and Fridays are its days; Navratri, Chaitra Navratri, and Durga Ashtami its festivals. The recitation runs about five minutes. Below you will find the complete prayoga in Devanagari Sanskrit, a transliteration in plain Latin script, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF to download for offline chanting.

She appears as a widow, riding a crow, holding a winnowing basket, and she is the seventh Mahavidya. The Dhumavati Stotram, ten verses from the Tantrasara, praises the goddess of smoke, of dissolution, of the moment misfortune comes to its end. The paradox at its heart is that this austere, impoverished form is the one invoked to drive out alakshmi, poverty and ill-fortune itself. The hymn is counted among the most powerful for clearing away calamities, enemies, and legal disputes, and for ending bad dreams, chronic illness, and the fear of old age and death. Saturdays and Tuesdays suit it, with Navratri and Amavasya as its occasions. Five minutes covers the recitation. The page offers the Devanagari text, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a downloadable PDF.

A thousand names, every cosmic form of the goddess folded into a single recitation. The Durga Sahasranama Stotram, drawn from the Skanda Purana, runs to twenty-six verses and sweeps through all her manifestations, the Dasha Mahavidyas and the Navadurga forms included, so that the whole of Shakti can be worshipped in one sitting. The tradition holds that reciting it at the three sandhyas fulfills every desire and destroys all sins, opening onto unmatched prosperity and, ultimately, moksha. Tuesdays and Fridays are kept for it, with Navratri and Diwali as the festival days. Set aside about thirty-five minutes for the full thousand. On this page the stotram is given in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

An elephant, seized by a crocodile and sinking, cries out at the last, and Vishnu comes. The Gajendra Moksha Stotram, sixteen verses from Canto 8 of the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, turns that moment of rescue into a prayer for divine intervention in one's own hour of crisis. It is recited to loosen the bondage of worldly attachments, the pasha-vimochana, to draw down the supreme compassion of Lord Vishnu, and to open the way to moksha and release from the cycle of birth and death. Thursdays and Sundays are its days, with Ekadashi and Vaikuntha Ekadashi as the festival occasions. The reading takes about ten minutes. You will find the complete stotram here in Devanagari Sanskrit, alongside a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a PDF to download for offline use.

Forty chaupais carry the name, since chalis means forty, and the framing dohas round the Ganga Chalisa out to forty-three verse-units in full recitation. The text comes from the traditional Chalisa Sangrah and is offered to Mother Ganga, the patit pavani who lifts the fallen. Devotees chant it for the purifying touch she is said to give, for merit equal to bathing in her waters, for the washing away of all sins, and for moksha, release from the wheel of rebirth. Monday and Thursday are the days kept for it, Purnima and Amavasya its sacred occasions. At forty-two verses the recitation takes around fifteen minutes. The page presents the whole Chalisa in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Where the Gayatri Mantra is a single seed, this hymn opens it into eleven verses of praise. The Gayatri Stotram comes from the Devi Bhagavatam and addresses Gayatri Mata as the mother of the Vedas, the source of light itself. The tradition turns to it to draw down her complete grace, to wash away every sin and purify the soul, to awaken brahma-jnana, the supreme knowledge, and to settle the mind in peace and spiritual illumination. Sunday is its day, and Gayatri Jayanti the festival that belongs to it above all. The eleven verses take about eight minutes to recite. On this page you can read the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, follow along with a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi arth, and download a free PDF for offline chanting.

When Krishna vanishes from the rasa dance, the gopis sing, and their song is the Gopi Geet. Nineteen verses from Canto 10, Chapter 31 of the Bhagavata Purana hold one of the most exalted prayers the tradition knows, the cry of love that longs for the Supreme without reserve. Reciting it is said to awaken prema-bhakti, the deepest form of divine love, to purify the heart and loosen material attachments, and to pour the nectar of krishna-katha into whoever listens. Janmashtami is the festival most fitting for it. The nineteen verses take about fifteen minutes to read through. Here you will find the Gopi Geet complete in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to sing along, the Hindi meaning explained, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

When Jupiter sits weak in a chart, this is the armor the tradition reaches for. The Guru Graha Kavacham, seven verses from the Graha Stotra Sangraha, is a protective kavach built to shield against afflictions tied to Brihaspati among the Navagraha. It is recited to sharpen wisdom and knowledge, to draw good fortune, to bring success in education, teaching, and spiritual pursuits, and to bless the home with progeny, wealth, and prosperity. Thursday is its day, the weekday ruled by Jupiter himself, and Guru Purnima the festival kept for it. The seven verses take about four minutes to recite. The page below carries the full kavacham in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline use.

Adi Shankaracharya set down five gems in praise of Hanuman, and they have been chanted at dawn ever since. The Hanuman Pancharatnam runs to five verses, each a jewel, and the tradition holds that reciting it at daybreak washes away all sins. Beyond that it is said to grant long life, good health, wealth, and strength, and to brighten the inner radiance and spiritual power of the one who chants it. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days, with Hanuman Jayanti as the festival occasion. The five verses take about five minutes to recite. On this page you can read the Pancharatnam in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the meaning in Hindi, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

From the first Mandala of the Rigveda comes a hymn to the king of the gods. The Indra Suktam, nine verses long, calls on Indra for strength and victory, the same power that once shattered Vritra. Reciting it is said to summon courage and valor when the way is hard, to throw up protection against enemies and adversaries, and to invoke the old Vedic link between Indra, the rains, prosperity, and the abundance of the fields. Thursday is the weekday kept for it. The nine verses take roughly six minutes to read. There is no festival fixed to it here, only the steady practice of chanting. The page gives you the suktam in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow along, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Tulsidas wrote it for a wedding, and weddings are still where it belongs. The Janaki Mangal, his own composition in eighteen verses, celebrates the marriage of Sita and Rama and is held to be especially auspicious for marriage ceremonies and proposals. Families recite it to clear the obstacles that stand between a person and a good life partner, to ask for marital harmony and a love that lasts, and to draw the joined blessings of both Sita and Rama. Fridays and Thursdays are its days, with Vivah Panchami and Ram Navami as the festivals kept for it. The eighteen verses run about twelve minutes. Below you will find the full text in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

The avatar yet to come has his own hymn. The Kalki Stotram, eleven verses from the Kalki Purana, looks ahead to the final descent of Vishnu and draws on that future protective power in the present age. Devotees recite it to cut away adharma and the negative forces at work in their own lives, to ask for protection through the darkness of Kali Yuga, and to hasten the dawn of Satya Yuga consciousness within. Thursday is its day, and Kalki Jayanti the festival that belongs to it. The eleven verses take about eight minutes to chant. On this page the stotram is laid out in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning explained verse by verse, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

The gopis chanted to her to win Krishna as a husband, and unmarried girls have chanted to her ever since. The Katyayani Stotram belongs to the Navdurga stotras of traditional worship and praises the sixth form of Durga, the one honored on Navratri Day 6. Its eleven verses are held to activate the Ajna chakra, the third eye, sharpening intuition and wisdom, and above all to grant a desired life partner and the marital happiness of vivaha sukha, in echo of that famous gopi mantra. Tuesdays and Fridays are its days, with Navratri as the festival occasion. The recitation takes about five minutes. The page carries the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline use.

One short hymn that tries to hold the whole of Krishna's leela. The Krishna Hridayam, eight verses from the Brahmavaivarta Purana, is the heart-essence prayer that moves from Bal-Krishna through the Krishna of Vrindavan to Dwarkadhish himself, and folds in the supreme Gita verse of surrender along the way. Chanting it is said to awaken prema-bhakti and draw down Krishna's grace, the whole sweep of his pastimes gathered into a single recitation. Wednesday is its day, with Janmashtami and Holi the festivals kept for it. The eight verses take about four minutes to chant. On this page you can read the Krishna Hridayam in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

Kunti asks Krishna for calamities, not comfort, because hardship keeps the mind turned toward him. Her prayer, the Kunti Stuti, comes from Canto 1, Chapter 8 of the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, and across eighteen verses it teaches the art of total surrender to God in the middle of adversity. Reciting it is said to call down Krishna's protection through every kind of danger, to reveal how calamity itself brings divine proximity, and to grow the vairagya that loosens one's grip on wealth and status. Wednesdays and Thursdays are its days, with Janmashtami and Ekadashi as the festival occasions. The eighteen verses run about twelve minutes. The page presents the full stuti in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline recitation.

Five jewel-verses, set down by Adi Shankaracharya, in praise of the goddess of wealth. The Lakshmi Pancharatnam is short by design, five verses meant to be chanted often, and the tradition turns to it for prosperity and good fortune in all their forms. Reciting it is said to lift away poverty and every kind of deprivation, and to draw an abundance of wealth, of grain, of resources into the home. Friday is its day, the weekday of Lakshmi, with Diwali, Sharad Purnima, and Akshaya Tritiya as the festivals that call for it. The five verses take only about three minutes, which makes them easy to fit into a daily habit. On this page the Pancharatnam appears in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

One verse from the Rigveda, at 7.59.12, and it is reckoned the most powerful Vedic mantra for facing down death and disease. The Mahamrityunjaya Mantra calls on the three-eyed Shiva, the fragrant one, to loosen us from death as a cucumber is loosed from its stem. Chanting it is said to bring longevity, good health, and freedom from chronic illness, to throw up protection against accidents, calamities, and untimely death, and to dissolve the fear of dying into fearlessness. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan as the high occasions for it. The single verse takes about three minutes when chanted with attention. The page carries the mantra in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline use.

Speech itself is the gift the ninth Mahavidya bestows. The Matangi Kavacham, eight verses from the Shakta Pramoda, raises the divine armor of Goddess Matangi around the one who chants it. The tradition holds it grants vak siddhi, the perfection of speech and eloquence, mastery in poetry, music, and all the arts, and proficiency in every vidya, so that the devotee stands victorious in any assembly. Fridays and Wednesdays are its days, with Navratri and Basant Panchami, the festival of learning, as its occasions. The eight verses take about seven minutes to recite. On this page you can read the kavacham in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

The flute-bearer of Vrindavan, praised in eight verses. The Muralidhara Stotram, from the Bhagavata Purana tradition, invokes the enchanting Muralidhara form of Krishna, the boy whose music drew the whole forest to him. Reciting it is said to settle the mind into a deep peace and calm through devotional meditation, to fill the heart with divine love and Krishna Prema, and it sits especially well with meditation on Krishna's Vrindavan pastimes. Wednesday is its day, with Janmashtami, Sharad Purnima, and Holi as the festivals kept for it. The eight verses take about five minutes to chant. The page offers the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to sing along with, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Nine forms of the goddess, each set as a guard over the body. The Navadurga Kavacham, fourteen verses from the Brahmanda Purana, builds a divine armor by calling on all nine forms of Durga at once, with each Navadurga form watching over a specific body part and direction. The tradition holds that, recited thrice daily, it drives off every enemy and removes all diseases, and that it wards against untimely death, the apamrityu, and every calamity. Tuesdays and Fridays are its days, with Navratri as the festival occasion. The fourteen verses take about eight minutes to recite. On this page the kavacham appears in full Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning explained, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Five of the most sacred Vedic hymns, bound into a single recitation. The Pancha Suktam draws from the Rig Veda, Yajur Veda, and Taittiriya Samhita, and gathers together the Purusha Suktam, the cosmic hymn of creation; the Sri Suktam, which calls on Lakshmi for wealth and prosperity; and the Bhu Suktam, which connects the chanter to Mother Earth for stability, among the five. Across its fourteen verses it is offered to Vishnu. Thursday is its day, with Ekadashi and Vaikuntha Ekadashi as the festival occasions kept for it. The full recitation takes about twelve minutes. The page below carries all fourteen verses in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow along, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline use.

Every form Mata Parvati takes, named one by one, a full thousand of them. The Parvati Sahasranama Stotram, ten verses from the Shiva Purana, sweeps through her manifestations, the Navadurga, the Dasha Mahavidya, and the Shodasha Matrika among them. Chanting it is said to draw all mangala, every kind of protection, and the full range of spiritual powers, and the tradition treats it as the supreme stotra for Navratri, Durga Puja, and Shivratri alike. Mondays and Fridays are its days, with Navratri, Maha Shivaratri, and Durga Puja as the festivals it belongs to. The ten verses take about eight minutes to recite. On this page you can read the stotram in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF for offline recitation.

Forty chaupais give the Chalisa its name, since chalis means forty, and the framing dohas bring the whole recitation to forty-three verse-units. The Radha Chalisa, from the traditional Chalisa Sangrah, is sung in praise of Radha Rani, the soul of Vrindavan. Devotees turn to it to deepen their love for the divine, to clear the obstacles that trouble matters of love and relationships, to bless married couples with a lasting harmony, and to ask Radha's grace for Krishna bhakti, the highest devotion. Wednesdays and Fridays are its days, with Radha Ashtami, Holi, and Janmashtami as the festivals kept for it. At forty-two verses the recitation runs about fourteen minutes. The page carries the full Chalisa in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download.

Twelve names hold the whole of Rama, son and husband, king and warrior, the divine being behind them all. The Rama Dwadash Nama Stotram, from the Valmiki Ramayana tradition and running to twelve verses, gathers these sacred names into a recitation simple enough for anyone yet weighty in its effect. The tradition holds that, chanted morning and evening, it carries away poverty and sorrow and brings sarva siddhi, success in all that one undertakes. Thursday is its day, with Ram Navami and Dussehra as the festivals it belongs to. The twelve verses take about four minutes to recite. On this page the stotram appears in full Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning laid out, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Three verses from the Yajur Veda, addressed to the fierce face of Shiva. The Rudra Gayatri is a Vedic meditation mantra that calls on the protective energy of Lord Rudra, and the tradition leans on it to clear away fear, negative energies, and evil influences. It is recited to ask for courage, strength, and fearlessness, and for shelter from untimely death and calamity. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and Pradosh as the occasions kept for it. The three verses are brief, about two minutes in all, which suits them to a daily round of chanting. The page below carries the mantra in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Sung on Fridays, when the goddess of contentment is worshipped, the Santoshi Mata Aarti is the eight-verse song at the center of her vrat. It comes down through tradition rather than any single scripture, and households turn to it for riddhi-siddhi, prosperity together with spiritual powers. Devotees sing it to lift away poverty and draw material abundance, and to ask for the blessing of children and family happiness. Friday is its day, and the Santoshi Mata Vrat the observance it belongs to above all. The eight verses take about five minutes to sing. On this page you can read the aarti in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation whenever the Friday worship comes around.

The goddess of learning, addressed by a full thousand names, the recitation opening with the dhyana shlokas. The Saraswati Sahasranama Stotram, twenty-five verses from the Skanda Purana, holds the famous Ya Kundendu meditation verse among its opening lines and then unfolds the full thousand names of Saraswati. Reciting it is said to grant eloquence, mastery of speech, and the divine vani, to open every form of knowledge, education, and scholarship, and to scatter ignorance, poverty, and all mental dullness. Thursday is its day, with Vasant Panchami and Navratri as the festivals kept for it. The twenty-five verses take about twenty-five minutes to recite. The page below carries the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow along, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

When famine struck, she grew vegetables from her own body to feed the starving, and the Shakambhari Stotram remembers it. Thirteen verses from the Devi Bhagavata Purana praise Shakambhari Devi, the nourisher, the form of Durga who makes food where there is none. Farmers and households recite it to clear away poverty, hunger, and every kind of scarcity, to ask for agricultural prosperity and an abundant harvest, and for protection against drought, famine, and natural calamity, so that, by her grace, the devotee never falls into destitution. Tuesdays and Fridays are its days, with Navratri and Shakambhari Navratri as the festivals it belongs to. The thirteen verses take about seven minutes to recite. On this page the stotram appears in Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download.

King Dasharatha once stood up to Saturn himself, and the stotra he is credited with became a shield against the planet's wrath. The Shani Vajrapanjara Stotram, ten verses from the Brahma Vaivarta Purana in the Dasharathakritam, builds a vajrapanjara, a diamond-cage, around the devotee against every Saturn affliction. Lord Brahma is said to have narrated it to the sages for humanity's sake, and it is held to guard every marma, every vital point of the body, from Shani's influence, while clearing away diseases, bondage, poverty, and the afflictions enemies cause. Saturday is its day, with Shani Jayanti as the festival kept for it. The ten verses take about six minutes to recite. The page carries the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF for offline use.

Mothers have sung this one over feverish children for generations. The Sheetala Mata Aarti, eight verses from the traditional Aarti Sangrah, is the song at the heart of the Sheetala Saptami, or Basoda, vrat, addressed to the goddess whose cooling energy guards against fevers, pox, and infectious disease. Families sing it to protect their children, to lift the fear of epidemics and contagion, and to draw health and wellness over the whole household. Mondays and Fridays are its days, with Sheetala Saptami and Basoda as the festivals it belongs to. The eight verses take about four minutes to sing. On this page you can read the aarti in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

A thousand names of Shiva, set down in the Mahabharata itself. The Shiv Sahasranama Stotram comes from the Anushasan Parva of that epic, which gives it unusual weight, and the tradition counts the chanting of these thousand names as the supreme form of his worship. Reciting them is said to carry the merit of visiting every Shiva temple, to wash away all sins and open the way to moksha, and to bestow health, wealth, long life, and spiritual knowledge. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri, the month of Shravan, and Pradosh as the occasions kept for it. The recitation, set across thirteen verse-units, takes about twenty-five minutes. The page below carries the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration, the Hindi arth, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

The sage Yajnavalkya composed it, and that authorship is part of why devotees trust its power. The Shiva Raksha Stotram, ten verses in the kavach style, sets Shiva's names as guards over each part of the body, building a complete protection around the one who chants it. The tradition holds that it lifts the fear of grahas, bhutas, pishacha, and every evil force, and that it grants both bhukti, worldly enjoyment, and mukti, liberation, together. Monday is its day, with Maha Shivaratri and the month of Shravan as the occasions for it. The ten verses take about six minutes to recite. On this page the stotram appears in full Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning explained, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Nathdwara's beloved form of Krishna has his own prayer in the Pushti Marg. The Shrinathji Stotram, eight verses from the Vallabha Sampradaya, invokes Shrinathji, the Govardhan-dhari who lifted the hill on his finger. Devotees of this tradition recite it for prosperity, devotion, and divine love, asking the grace of the Pushti Marg to awaken krishna-prema in the heart. Wednesdays, Fridays, and Sundays are its days, with Janmashtami, Govardhan Puja, Holi, and Annakut as the festivals it belongs to. The eight verses take about eight minutes to recite. The page below carries the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow along, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

A thousand names trace Sita through her whole life, from Mithila to the forest and back. The Sita Sahasranama Stotram, ten verses from the Ananda Ramayana, praises Mata Sita as the incarnation of Mahalakshmi and gathers the names of her complete journey into one recitation. Chanting it is said to grant supreme saubhagya and marital harmony, and to draw the pativrata shakti and the family blessings that flow from it. Friday is its day, with Sita Navami, Vivah Panchami, and Ram Navami as the festivals kept for it. The ten verses take about seven minutes to recite. On this page you can read the stotram in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi meaning, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

Twelve names of the Sun, the ones traditionally chanted through the rounds of Surya Namaskar. The Surya Dvadash Nama Stotram draws on the Surya Upanishad and the Ishavasya Upanishad, and its eight verses carry the Vedic Ishavasya mantra for Surya worship within them. Recited at sunrise, it is said to clear away diseases and sorrows, and to grant longevity, intelligence, strength, vigor, and radiance. Sunday is its day, though it is also chanted daily, with Makar Sankranti, Ratha Saptami, and Chhath Puja as the festivals kept for it. The eight verses take about four minutes to recite. The page below carries the full stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

She is the one who ferries souls across the ocean of existence, the second of the Mahavidyas. The Tara Stotram, ten verses from the Tantrasara, praises Tara, also called Neel Saraswati, the goddess who grants all knowledge and wisdom, and whom Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva themselves are said to have worshipped. Reciting it is held to scatter the darkness of ignorance, the avidya, with the light of vidya, and to carry the devotee safely over. Tuesdays and Saturdays are its days, with Navratri and Kali Puja as the festivals kept for it. The ten verses take about five minutes to recite. On this page the stotram appears in full Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Uma is Parvati in her gentlest aspect, and this hymn from the Shiva Purana sings to her in eight verses. The Uma Stuti is recited for her supreme grace, and the tradition holds that it carries the devotee past the fear of the bhava-sagara, the ocean of worldly existence. It is chanted, too, for spiritual elevation and the tapas siddhi that comes of austerity, and for the all-round auspiciousness and fulfillment associated with her. Mondays and Fridays are its days, with Navratri as the festival occasion. The eight verses take about seven minutes to recite. On this page you can read the stuti in full Devanagari Sanskrit, follow a Latin transliteration, study the Hindi meaning verse by verse, and download a free PDF to keep for offline recitation.

The Taittiriya Upanishad addresses the wind-god directly: Tvameva Pratyaksham Brahma, you alone are the visible Brahman. The Vayu Ashtakam takes up that declaration across eight verses drawn from the Vayu Purana and the Taittiriya Upanishad, praising Vayu as Brahman made manifest. Reciting it is said to bring vital energy and strength and freedom from respiratory ailments, and it invokes Vayu as the father of both Hanuman and Bhima, the source of their great strength. Saturday is the weekday kept for it; no festival is fixed to it here. The eight verses take about four minutes to recite. The page below carries the full ashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit, a Latin transliteration to follow along, the Hindi arth for meaning, and a free PDF download for offline recitation.

Narayanaya Vidmahe, the opening line runs, reaching for the supreme preserver. The Vishnu Gayatri Mantra, from the Rig Veda and the Vishnu Purana, gathers five verses and three variations covering the Narayana, Shrinivasa, and Chaturbhuja forms of the Lord. Chanting it is held to wash away all sins and grant a permanent abode in Vaikuntha, and it is reckoned strong for spiritual upliftment and divine protection alike. Thursday is its day, with Ekadashi and Vaikuntha Ekadashi as the festivals kept for it. The five verses take about four minutes to recite. On this page the mantra appears in full Devanagari Sanskrit, with a Latin transliteration to follow, the Hindi meaning laid out, and a free PDF you can download for offline recitation.

Krishna's own river has her own hymn. The Yamuna Stotram, six verses from traditional Sanskrit hymnody, praises the Yamuna as the beloved river of Krishna and turns to her for his special grace. Bathing in her, the tradition says, purifies body and mind, clears away skin diseases and all sins, and the stotra carries that promise into recitation, said also to secure residence in Vishnu-loka after death and to grant devotion to Krishna along with spiritual knowledge. Wednesday is its day, with Yamuna Chhath and Janmashtami as the festivals kept for it. The six verses take about six minutes to recite.
